title: Getting Started with Git
content:
### Checking Git Version

Verify Git is installed and see the version:

```bash
git --version
```

### Configuring Git

Set your identity — every commit uses this info:

```bash
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"
```

Configuration levels:
- `--system` — all users on the machine
- `--global` — all repos for your user
- `--local` — current repo only (default)

### Creating a Repository

```bash
git init
```

This creates a new Git repo in the current directory. The default branch name is `main` (configurable with `init.defaultBranch`).

### Checking Status

```bash
git status
```

Shows which files are modified, staged, or untracked. Use `-s` for a short format.

### The .git Folder

When you run `git init`, Git creates a `.git` subdirectory containing all repo metadata:
- `HEAD` — points to current branch
- `config` — repo-specific config
- `objects/` — all data (commits, trees, blobs)
- `refs/` — pointers to commits (branches, tags)
- `hooks/` — client-side scripts
codeExample:
git --version
git config --global user.name "Ada Lovelace"
git config --global user.email "ada@example.com"
mkdir my-project && cd my-project
git init
git status

quiz.question: Which command configures Git identity for all repos on your machine?
